findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've identified a real market problem and sized it well, but investors will want to know you've actually talked to the people who need to solve it—add a slide showing 3-4 specific customer conversations (even informal ones count: "Spoke with 12 villa developers in Dubai, 8 cited reliability as top blocker to solar adoption" or "Got 3 letters of intent from property management companies willing to pilot"). This transforms your mandate-driven TAM into proof that people will actually buy.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist rather than a forcing function—add one concrete timeline that shows why developers can't wait. For example: "Estidama Phase 3 (effective Q3 2025) requires solar hot water on all new villas, but 80% of current systems fail inspection within 18 months, forcing costly retrofits—developers need a compliant alternative now or face project delays and fines." This turns "tightening regulations" into an urgent deadline that makes your solution timely, not just relevant.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature roadmap, not a defensible technology. Add one concrete example showing how your AI actually works: for instance, "HelioHelp ingests building orientation, roof load capacity, and local water hardness data, then outputs a custom collector array configuration and predicted 25-year performance curve — something installers previously needed 2-3 weeks of engineering to calculate." This shows investors the actual intelligence layer, not just the promise of automation.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: You're building a platform that depends on AI working reliably at scale, but you haven't mentioned what cloud infrastructure, APIs, or third-party services power HelioHelp — this matters to investors who worry about vendor lock-in or service interruptions. Add a brief line to Slide 19 stating something like "HelioHelp runs on [AWS/Azure/GCP] with redundancy across [regions]" and note any critical third-party dependencies (e.g., weather APIs for solar modeling, mapping services) so investors know your scaling plan isn't hostage to a single provider's uptime.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi, GCC) with mandated solar installations and utility partnerships, but investors won't see how you're handling data security, system certifications, or compliance requirements—especially critical since HelioHelp collects building data and performance metrics globally. Add a single bullet on Slide 5 or 8 stating your compliance posture: "ISO/EN certified manufacturing + [specific standard for IoT/data handling, e.g., ISO 27001 roadmap or regional compliance framework] for HelioHelp deployment" so it's clear you've thought through the regulatory landscape your partners care about.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides present financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a single "Assumptions" box to your financials slide that shows the three drivers behind your Base case: units sold per year (starting with your 50-installation target by end of 2026, then scaling to X units in 2027), average hardware revenue per unit (e.g., $15K-$20K based on your Rixos deal at $207K for 570 collectors), and gross margin progression (35-45% hardware, with licensing upside). This transforms "conservative projections" from a claim into a transparent, testable model that investors can actually stress-test themselves.

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see CAC and LTV to understand if your unit economics actually work at scale—especially for the villa segment where you're chasing 500+ unit deals. Add a simple table showing: for a typical villa installation, what's your all-in customer acquisition cost (sales, installation, marketing), what's the lifetime value from that customer (hardware margin + O&M recurring revenue over 10 years), and the ratio—this will prove your model isn't just profitable on paper but repeatable across your pipeline deals like the Masdar City retrofit program.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy of $87,000 between the contracted amount and projected hardware revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted value on Slide 12 needs to flow into your 2026 hardware revenue on Slide 14—right now it shows $0.3M ($300K), which creates confusion about whether the Rixos and EtihadWE deals are already baked into that number or separate. Add a footnote to the 2026 hardware revenue stating "Includes $213K contracted deployments (Rixos Hotel, EtihadWE); remaining $87K from pipeline conversion," so investors see the contracted work as the foundation of your 2026 forecast, not a gap.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated "Use of Funds" slide that breaks down exactly how you'll deploy this new capital over the next 18 months—for example: "Manufacturing setup & inventory: $120K | Sales & BD team: $80K | Pilot support & installation: $100K | Working capital: $50K" tied directly to hitting your 50-installation target and Q2 2026 deployment milestones you've already outlined on Slide 12.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to the Rixos and EtihadWE bullets stating the payment status — something like "Rixos (live project, $X revenue received)" or "EtihadWE (contract signed, payment terms: X)." This transforms them from project names into proof points that money has actually changed hands, which is what investors need to see to believe you have real traction.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: You've got great partnerships and unit economics here, but investors need to see actual growth momentum—add specific numbers showing how revenue or units sold have grown month-over-month or year-over-year (for example: "Direct sales grew from $120K in Q1 to $280K in Q2" or "Units shipped increased 45% MoM from March to April"). This proves the model is working, not just theoretically sound.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos that's been running your system for over a year—that's gold, so show it. Add a single metric to your Rixos case study: something like "System uptime: 99.2% | Customer renewal: Signed 570-unit expansion PO" or "NPS: 9/10 | Retention: 100% (pilot → full deployment)." This turns your best proof point into evidence that customers actually stick around and trust you enough to bet big.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Name the actual competitors in your GCC market instead of generic categories—replace "Legacy Solar" with something like "Solahart or Rheem flat-plate systems" and "Heat Pumps" with "Daikin or Carrier split units," so investors immediately understand who you're beating and can verify your claims against real products they might know.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why SolarisKit wins against existing products, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Carrier, or a well-funded solar company decides to build exactly what you're building. Add a "Competitive Moat" section that explains what makes your solution defensible—for example, is it your proprietary polymer absorber material that takes 18 months to develop and patent, your specific engineering for GCC flat-roof codes that competitors would need to recertify, or your existing relationships with Gulf installers that create switching costs? Pick the one or two that are genuinely hard to replicate and make that explicit.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors believe you'll execute—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and bring credibility in areas your team doesn't cover (like fundraising experience, enterprise sales at scale, or regulatory/certification expertise for thermal systems). For example, if you're entering new markets, name an advisor who's successfully scaled hardware in those regions, or if manufacturing is critical, bring in someone from a tier-one supplier who can validate your supply chain strategy.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders and any equity cliffs — for example, "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" — so investors immediately see you're aligned for the long haul and there's no ambiguity about who stays committed through execution.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification and patents locked down, but investors in regulated markets will want to see explicit alignment with the three mandates you mention—Estidama, Dubai, and Barjeel—spelled out on your compliance slide (Appendix B). Add a one-line summary for each showing which standard your system meets (e.g., "Estidama: Meets mandatory solar thermal efficiency tier 2 for residential buildings") so it's crystal clear you're not just compliant but ahead of the curve on the regulations driving your market.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked down and a solid risk mitigation slide, but investors will want to know upfront whether there are any existing patent challenges, licensing disputes, or claims against your IP—especially since you're entering a competitive market with heat pump and PV alternatives already established. Add a one-sentence statement to your IP/Defensibility slide (Slide 8) that explicitly confirms: "No pending litigation, IP disputes, or third-party claims against HelioFlow or HelioHelp patents" — this removes ambiguity and builds confidence that your moat is clean and defensible.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these align. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing how the 5% UAE market share ($20M) connects to your $400M SAM — for example: "5% UAE market share of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity" or show the calculation explicitly so investors see the math is intentional, not disconnected. Right now it reads like two separate claims, and investors will pause to verify the logic themselves.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, and Slide 3 labeled '[UNKNOWN]' suggests incomplete slide deck
findings.suggestion: Go through the entire deck systematically and replace every [URL_REDACTED] placeholder with the actual URL or remove the reference entirely—investors will notice these gaps immediately and assume the deck isn't final. For Slide 6, finish the cut-off sentence "Strong push for dec" (it should read "Strong push for decarbonisation" based on context), and rename Slide 3 from "[UNKNOWN]" to "Solution" to match your actual content. A quick 10-minute pass will make this feel polished and ready.
